exemplar.title: To what extent does the type and severity of schizophrenic symptoms correlate with the inclanation towards violent behavior

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does the type and severity of schizophrenic symptoms correlate with the inclanation towards violent behavior

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: The research question is clearly stated, narrow in scope, and consistently anchors the investigation, ensuring sustained focus.
Topic communication is accurate and effective throughout, with each section directly linked back to the central line of inquiry.
Methodology selection shows evidence of informed choice (e.g., CATIE trial, twin studies) and relevance to the research question.
weaknesses: Minor background digressions (etymology of âschizophreniaâ) detract slightly from cohesion and could be streamlined.
Use of popularâscience websites and general information pages reduces overall scholarly rigour.
Reliance largely on secondary synthesis rather than primary data analysis limits methodological depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation communicates its purpose and focus clearly and precisely, and the research question is both explicitly stated and tightly integrated throughout. Methodologically, the student demonstrates mostly relevant source selection and critiques key empirical studies, but the reliance on lay sources and secondary summaries prevents placement in the highest band. Overall, performance across the three strands merits a high mark of 5 out of 6.

strengths: Terminology and concepts (e.g., âcommand hallucinations,â âsingleâblind RCT,â âmedication nonâadherenceâ) are used accurately and consistently.
Explanation of symptom clusters and antipsychotic mechanisms demonstrates good subject knowledge.
Application of empirical findings (e.g., twin heritability, linking command hallucinations to violence) shows clear understanding.
weaknesses: Frequent reliance on nonâacademic sources (Mayo Clinic, Verywell Mind) weakens academic rigour and depth.
Some claims (neurotransmitter pathways, social context factors) lack engagement with primary peer-reviewed literature.
Evidence application is uneven: certain contextual or adherence issues are supported by only a single study rather than meta-analyses.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work exhibits good knowledge and mostly relevant application of sources, with consistently accurate use of disciplinary terminology. However, reliance on general information sites and occasional oversimplifications keep the performance out of the top band. A score of 5 out of 6 appropriately reflects strong but not exemplary Knowledge and Understanding.

strengths: The essay draws on a range of relevant studies (epidemiological data, RCTs, twin studies) that directly inform the research question.
Analysis links evidence and claims coherently, synthesising symptom types and risk factors into a layered explanation.
Discussion includes critique of methodological limitations (e.g., self-report bias, accessibility of AVATAR therapy) and considers ethical constraints.
weaknesses: Depth of evaluation is limited by omission of quantitative metrics (effect sizes, p-values) and twinâmodel statistical explanations.
Alternative interpretations and counterarguments are only briefly acknowledged, reducing critical balance.
Conceptual distinctions (external factors vs symptom severity) could be more rigorously separated, and demographic generalisability is under-examined.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates solid critical thinking with good research relevance, clear analysis, and reasoned evaluation. Multiple critiques of study quality and synthesis of multifactorial influences show strong performance, but the lack of deeper quantitative evaluation and fuller consideration of alternative perspectives prevents placement in the top third of the band. A mid-band score of 6 out of 12 accurately reflects these strengths and weaknesses.

strengths: The report follows a clear structure with title page, table of contents, section headings, and logical progression.
Layout elements (font, spacing, citation style) are applied consistently, with word count and page numbers correctly presented.
Presentation conventions are largely met, enabling smooth navigation of the document.
weaknesses: Page numbers and dot-leader formatting in the table of contents are misaligned, hindering navigability.
Sub-heading formatting is inconsistent, and the conclusion is incomplete, disrupting professional polish.
Bibliographic details are occasionally incomplete and some hyperlinks are broken.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation is competent: structure and formatting conventions are observed, and the document is generally clear and navigable. However, minor lapses in tableâofâcontents alignment, heading consistency, and bibliography completeness prevent a top-band mark. A score of 3 out of 4 reflects effective but slightly imperfect presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: Terminology such as **âcommand hallucinations,â âpharmacotherapy,â âsingle-blind RCT,â âheritability,â** and **âmedication non-adherenceâ** is used accurately and consistently. Concepts are explained correctly and applied in context; there are only occasional lapses in statistical phrasing.  Overall, the language reflects a strong and confident grasp of psychological vocabulary.

Layout is generally consistent: font, spacing and citation style are applied uniformly, the word count is stated, and pages are numbered.  Nevertheless, several references lack complete bibliographic details and some hyperlinks are broken or inconsistently formatted, slightly undermining professional polish.  These minor lapses keep the work within the competent (3-4) band rather than the highest band.

The student demonstrates **good subject knowledge**, accurately explaining positive, negative and cognitive symptom clusters, heritability figures, and the mechanism of antipsychotics.  Empirical work is used appropriately to illustrate points, yet the depth of engagement with peer-reviewed literature is uneven and some claims (e.g., neurotransmitter pathways) rest on lay sources.  This places the work solidly in the âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ category.

A generally appropriate methodology is evident: key empirical studies (twin study, CATIE data, MacArthur study, AVATAR RCT) are selected and critiqued, showing awareness of sample sizes, controls and limitations.  However, the use of several popular-science websites (Verywell Mind, Medical News Today) and general information pages (Mayo Clinic) reduces scholarly rigour, and the essay relies largely on secondary synthesis rather than primary data analysis.  Hence methodology is mostly but not fully complete.

The student communicates the focus on the link between schizophrenic symptom profiles and violent behaviour **clearly and precisely from the title onward**. Each main section (symptoms, risk factors, treatment) remains anchored to this purpose and the research question is repeatedly referenced, ensuring the reader never loses sight of the central line of inquiry.  Minor digressions into etymology or historical background are brief and do not seriously impede coherence, so the overall communication remains accurate, effective, and sustained.

The essay draws on a **range of relevant research**âlarge-scale epidemiological work, clinical trials, and an innovative therapy studyâeach directly linked to the question.  Source selection is largely appropriate and clearly connected to points under discussion; weaknesses (non-academic sites) are present but do not dominate, so relevance remains high overall.

The research question is explicitly stated on the title page and is **narrow enough (severity /type of specific symptoms vs violence) to be investigated within 4 000 words**.  Throughout the essay the question is consistently used to organise material (e.g., subdivision of personal, historical, contextual, and clinical factors), demonstrating firm alignment between the investigation and the guiding focus.  No substantial drift or over-breadth is evident.

The essay follows standard EE structure with title page, abstract substitute, table of contents, section headings, and clear progression from introduction to conclusion.  Minor issuesâmisaligned page numbers in the contents and occasional inconsistent sub-heading formattingâprevent a fully seamless presentation, but overall the structure is appropriate and functional.

The discussion synthesises findings into a reasoned argument that positive symptoms, moderated by external factors, elevate violence risk.  Some evaluation of study quality and ethical constraints is provided, yet alternative interpretations (e.g., socio-legal perspectives or protective factors) are only briefly acknowledged.  The argument remains coherent but could be more critically balanced, placing it in the upper part of the 4-6 band.

Analytical links between evidence and claims are generally clear: the student contrasts twin concordance figures with environmental moderators and compares Buchanan and MacArthur findings to build a layered explanation.  Critique of study limitations (self-report bias, cultural specificity, resource demands of AVATAR therapy) shows engagement, but discussion of effect sizes, statistical power, and contradictory data is limited.  Consequently the analysis is solid but not consistently incisive.

annotations.comment: The research question is explicitly stated, narrow in scope, and directly aligned with the investigationâs purpose, providing clear focus for the entire essay.
The word count is provided and falls within the 4000-word limit, satisfying the formatting requirement for the extended essay.
Citing a popular online etymology site for scholarly context weakens academic rigour; replace with peer-reviewed linguistic sources or eliminate this citation.
Relying on a Mayo Clinic overview for neurotransmitter mechanisms oversimplifies complex pathways; refer to primary neuroscience literature for detailed explanations.
The identification of study strengths (large twin sample, use of national registers) and limitations (geographical scope) reflects good analytical understanding of study validity.
Page numbers in the contents table appear misaligned and formatted unevenly; ensure consistent tab stops or leader dots to improve navigability.
The discussion of the word origins of âschizophreniaâ is an interesting background but detracts from the central focus; consider streamlining or relocating this etymology to maintain cohesion.
The neurobiological explanation is descriptive but relies on a general Internet source (Cherry, 2019); integrate peer-reviewed studies to deepen subject knowledge and source application.
The title is clearly stated and effectively communicates the investigation focus, meeting the IB requirement for a concise and descriptive title at the start of the report.
Linking command hallucinations to impulsive violence shows strong application of subject knowledge and relevance to the research question.
MacArthur study description omits discussion of matching procedures and potential confounders in the comparison group; evaluate representativeness more critically.
Findings are summarised but lack effect sizes or p-values; include quantitative metrics to support claims about violence rates among patients.
The dot-leader formatting and spacing in the table of contents are inconsistent, making it harder to match section titles with their page numbers; adjust for uniform alignment.
The evocative Elyn R. Saks quotation lacks critical integration; discuss how this anecdote informs the research question rather than serving as narrative embellishment.
Heritability is presented as a direct percentage without explaining the twin-model statistical methods; clarify how the 79% figure was derived to strengthen analysis depth.
The discussion of external factors versus symptom severity conflates constructs; establish clearer conceptual separation and causal pathways.
Nonadherence effects are described using a single study; broaden source application by including systematic reviews or meta-analyses on medication adherence.
Resource and accessibility limitations of AVATAR therapy are noted, but the essay does not propose strategies for broader implementation; consider discussing scalability.
The final summary reiterates earlier conclusions without adding new insight, resulting in redundancy; refine to improve flow and avoid repetition.
Works Cited entries display inconsistent bibliographic details and several broken hyperlinks; standardise formatting and verify link functionality.
Citing Mayo Clinic for neurotransmitter roles fails to engage with current peer-reviewed research; enhance knowledge demonstration by including recent journal studies.
Terminology distinguishes cognitive, positive, and negative symptom clusters accurately, demonstrating consistent and precise use of disciplinary concepts.
The negative symptom section is descriptive but omits prevalence or severity metrics; consider integrating empirical data for richer analysis.
Selection of the CATIE trial demonstrates informed methodology choice and relevance, aligning study design with the research question effectively.
Noting the self-report limitation is good, but the critique lacks depth regarding bias impact; quantify potential reporting errors or cite alternative measures.
Integration of childhood trauma as a historical risk factor demonstrates nuanced understanding of multifactorial influences on violence.
Claims regarding social support and status lack empirical citation; incorporate sociological or epidemiological data to substantiate contextual factor statements.
Sample description lacks assessment of demographic representativeness and generalisability; discuss how age range and locale may limit applicability.
Treatment categories are listed descriptively but lack evidence-based evaluation of their effectiveness; strengthen with outcome data from clinical trials.
Detailing AVATAR therapyâs single-blind RCT design and participant criteria demonstrates awareness of research quality and methodological relevance.
The discussion effectively synthesises key findings across studies, linking symptom types and risk factors into a coherent argument about violence risk.
The concluding paragraph is incomplete and trails off, which disrupts the presentation; ensure all final points are fully articulated.
